CSCE 726 Syllabus

Section 600 (58445)

Large-Scale Optimization for Machine Learning

Spring 2026 - College Station

Course Information

Course Number: CSCE-726

Course Title: Large-scale Optimization for Machine Learning

Section: Spring 2026

Course Website: CANVAS

Time:  Monday, Wednesday, Friday (3:00-3:50pm)

Location: H.R. Bright Building, Room 126

Credit Hours: 3

Credit Hours:  3

Instructor Details

Tianbao Yang

Email:  tianbao-yang@tamu.edu

Office Location:  436

Phone:  9798458873

Office Hours

Monday 4-5pm

Catalog Description

Large-Scale Optimization for Machine Learning. (3-0). Credit 3. Fundamental

optimization algorithms and their analysis for solving machine learning problems;

deterministic and stochastic gradient-based algorithms; adaptive gradient-based

methods; momentum-based methods; convergence analysis of selected algorithms;

implementation of algorithms and their application to solving of real-world machine

learning and artificial intelligence problems. Prerequisites: CSCE 633 or CSCE 636 or

approval of instructor; graduate classification.

Additional Course Details

Large-scale optimization plays an important role in machine learning (ML) and

artificial intelligence (AI). In the past twenty years, there are many efficient

Additional Course Details

Large-scale optimization plays an important role in machine learning (ML) and

artificial intelligence (AI). In the past twenty years, there are many efficient

algorithms that have been proposed and employed for solving various machine

learning problems, e.g., stochastic gradient descent (SGD), adaptive gradient methods

(e.g., AdaGrad, Adam), momentum-based methods, etc. Many of these algorithms

have been implemented in modern deep learning packages such as Pytorch and

Tensorflow.

This course will cover fundamental optimization algorithms and their analysis for

solving advanced machine learning problems. It will cover advanced machine

learning methods and classic and modern stochastic gradient-based algorithms.

Students will not only need to understand the fundamental principle of machine

learning and convergence analysis of various optimization algorithms but also need to

implement them through Python and apply them for solving real-world ML/AI

problems. Prerequisites include:

You should be comfortable with intensive Math and Programming.

Basic knowledge about optimization (e.g., convex functions, strongly convex

functions, gradient, etc.) and machine learning (e.g., supervised learning, linear

regression, logistic regression, support vector machines, basic deep learning).

Math Background: Linear algebra, Calculus, Probability and Statistics, Optimization

regression, logistic regression, support vector machines, basic deep learning).

Math Background: Linear algebra, Calculus, Probability and Statistics, Optimization

Programming Background: Python, Pytorch, Tensorflow

Preferably access to GPUs (e.g.,  Google Colab ,  TAMU HPRC )

Course Prerequisites

Prerequisite/Corequisite(s):  CSCE 633 or CSCE 636 or approval of instructor;

graduate classification.

Recommended Background

Proficiency

in

Math

(linear

algebra,

probability/statistics,

optimization).  Half of homework assignments will be written questions about

deriving some mathematical proofs for analyzing some machine learning

problems and optimization algorithms. You should be comfortable of

mathematical analysis involving functions, gradients, inequalities, matrices,

vectors, random variables, expectations, etc.

Proficiency in Python . Half of homework assignments will be programming

questions. You are required to implement some optimization algorithms in

Python and apply them to solve some ML/AI problems.

Familiar with PyTorch . Some coding assignments involving deep learning

will use  PyTorch , which is an excellent python-based toolbox for machine

learning and deep learning. Check out the official  tutorial .

Some Foundations of  Machine Learning   and   Deep Learning .  You should be

familiar with some basic machine learning methods/techniques, e.g.,

Some Foundations of  Machine Learning   and   Deep Learning .  You should be

familiar with some basic machine learning methods/techniques, e.g.,

linear regression, logistic regression, support vector machine,

regularization, and have some basic knowledge of deep learning. You

will need to set up a deep neural network and run some optimization

algorithms for learning the parameters of the deep neural network on

some large-scale datasets, and conduct the evaluations.

Recommended Background Courses.  CSCE 110 Programming,  MATH 304 Linear

Algebra , MATH 411 Mathematical Probability,  CSCE 625/425 Artificial Intelligence ,

CSCE 633/ 421  Machine Learning,  CSCE 636 Deep Learning .

Course Learning Outcomes

The objective of this course is to teach some classic deterministic optimization and

modern large-scale stochastic optimization algorithms for solving ML/AI problems.

Upon completion of the course, students will:

1.  be able to name classical stochastic optimization algorithms and analyze their

convergence properties;

2.  be able to evaluate and compare the performance of different optimization

algorithms for solving the same problem;

3.  be able to apply tuning strategies to improve the performance of an optimization

algorithm;

4.  be able to design some new optimization algorithms for solving large-

3.  be able to apply tuning strategies to improve the performance of an optimization

algorithm;

4.  be able to design some new optimization algorithms for solving large-

scale ML problems .

The learning outcomes will be assessed based on homework assignments and a final

course project.

Special Course Designation

None

Textbook and/or Resource Materials

This material Is: Required

Compositional Optimization for Advanced Machine Learning

Authors:  Tianbao Yang

Notes:

https://opt4ml.org/

Grading Policy

Grading Policy

Homework assignments: 45%

Project: 55% (Proposal 10%, Presentation 15%, Final Results 30%)

Grading Scale

A = [90, 100]

B = [80, 90)

C = [70, 80)

D = [60, 70)

F = <60

Grading Disputes

Questions on assignment grading should be discussed with the instructor. Grading

dispute period for the course project will be determined and announced at the time of

grade posting based on university deadline for submitting letter grades. Grading

disputes for all other graded materials (other than the course project) must be

presented to the respective party within ONE week upon receiving grading results

(timed as email notification). All grades after the dispute time windows are

considered final.

Late Work Policy

Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not

considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy ( Student Rule 7 ).

considered final.

Late Work Policy

Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not

considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy ( Student Rule 7 ).

Course Specific Late Work Policy

Late Policies

Late submission caused by excused absence (defined in  Student rule 7 ) will be

accepted. Excused absence will not be counted towards late days. Contact the

instructor if you think you have an extremely rare circumstance for which we should

make an exception. This policy is to ensure that feedback can be given in a timely

manner.

Unexpected excused absences: In cases where prior notification of excused absence is

not feasible (e.g., accident or emergency) the student must provide notification by the

end of the second working day after the absence, including an explanation of why

notice could not be sent prior to the class.

Each homework assignment due by midnight (11:59pm) on the due date. Late

homeworks will not be graded so please just hand in whatever you have completed.

Solution sketches will be provided after homeworks have been turned in. Late project

submissions will not be accepted.

AI Statement

The use of AI tools such as large language models for completing homework

assignments, exams, or the course project is strictly prohibited. However, it is

acceptable to use them for tasks such as editing your writing or assisting in debugging

your code.
